steps.text: Determine whether the licensee’s fuel handling procedures are adequate to perform intended functions.
Determine whether fuel is moved and inspected consistent with the requirements of the TS and the licensee’s procedures.
Determine whether fuel handling activities are conducted in accordance with 10 CFR Part 20 and the licensee’s procedures and programs for radiation protection.
Determine whether the licensee satisfied security plan requirements for fuel movement activities.
Determine whether significant fuel movement or inspection problems are identified and resolved in accordance with the licensee’s procedural controls.
Determine whether the licensee was within TS limits and met procedural requirements before resuming normal operation after fuel movement.
Transfer fuel into a core configuration, or to racks or containers designed to prevent accidental criticality.
Inspect fuel and control rods in accordance with the TS and their procedures.
Check the control rods to determine their capability to continue to function.
Inspect installed neutron source for leakage paths if removed during refueling.
Monitor or sample for airborne radioactive particulate matter.
Monitor exits from controlled areas.
Perform surveys for contamination in accordance with licensee procedures.
Monitor radiation areas in accordance with Subpart F of 10 CFR Part 20.
Check the set points of continuous area radiation monitors.
Survey periodically to determine whether actual radiation levels are consistent with area radiation monitors.
Use personnel dosimetry as specified by the licensee’s procedures.
Check direct reading dosimeters frequently.
Observe security controls during one fuel movement activity.
Review records and discuss with responsible personnel for the most recent fuel movement activity.
Ensure new fuel is kept in the security area and locked in a fuel vault.
Observe the resolution of one fuel movement problem.
Review licensee’s logs and records regarding identified problems.
Observe one reactor startup after fuel movement.
Review respective licensee logs and records for the most recent fuel movement activity.
Check changes to core configuration, calibration of control rods, and determination of excess reactivity and shutdown margin.

preconditions.text: If direct observation is not possible, review radiation protection records and discuss with responsible personnel.

constraints.text: Safe fuel handling includes assurance that the fuel is not damaged, that accidental criticality does not occur, and that radiation and contamination hazards are minimized.
Participants must be trained and understand their tasks.
Dry runs may be necessary for operations of potential safety concern or of an unusual nature.
Minimum critical volume varies and specific moderation, enrichment, poison, and core structure may allow much less than a cubic yard of typical fuel and water to have a critical mass.
The licensee must have provisions to inspect the neutron source for leakage paths such as splitting or pitting.
Maintain occupational doses as low as reasonably achievable (ALARA).
Surveys are required to be performed in accordance with licensee procedures.
